revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Suzu, Ishikawa]]

revision.comment: Moved from [[Suzu, Ishikawa]]. [[Suzu, Ishikawa]] reverted to article about the place.
revision.text:
A '''suzu''' is a [[Shinto]] religious bell charm, similar to the [[omamori]]. Some depict gods and heroes while other designs such as lotuses are inspired by nature. Another common motif is the Shinto symbol mitsu [[tomoe]]. Gods available in suzus include Surutahiko, Ame-no-Uzume, Amaterasu, Inari, and Hotei. The suzu is usually bronze in color but can be silver, gold, or red. Suzus offer luck and proectection and address different prayers. Many Shintoists cary suzus with them when they are away from a shrine or home altar called a [[kamidana]]. They are ofter worn on the wrist or carried by purse/pocket.
[[Category:Shinto]]

revision.comment: Some small spelling and article use corrections
revised by a correct use in Japan.
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Suzu (disambiguation)}} [[Image:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|Suzu hang under the eaves of the Shinto shrine]] A {{nihongo|'''''suzu'''''|鈴}} is a ball-shaped [[Shinto]] religious bell. Ringing a handheld clustered suzu like [[Jingle bell|sleigh bells for musical instrument]], is same as waving a {{nihongo|[[gohei]]|御幣}}, means calling gods and divinized heroes; then it gets their spiritual power; so over persons means giving their authority and power to them, or against evils for dispersing.
Therefore suzu itself came to be believed to have spiritual power, then small one became a bell charm called {{nihongo|[[omamori]]|お守り}} bestowed by the Shinto shrines.
Ringing suzu hang under the eaves of the Shinto shrine, means like a doorbell for gods. In this case, another flat-shaped one without clapper but mallet, called {{nihongo|waniguchi|鰐口}}(literaly crocodile mouth) is also used.
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Bells]]
{{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:鈴]]

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Suzu (disambiguation)}} [[Image:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|Suzu hang under the eaves of the Shinto shrine]] A {{nihongo|'''''suzu'''''|鈴}} is a ball-shaped [[Shinto]] religious bell. Ringing a handheld clustered suzu like [[Jingle bell|sleigh bells for musical instrument]], is same as waving a {{nihongo|[[gohei]]|御幣}}, means calling gods and divinized heroes; then it gets their spiritual power; so over persons means giving their authority and power to them, or against evils for dispersing.
Therefore suzu itself came to be believed to have spiritual power, then small one became a bell charm called {{nihongo|[[omamori]]|お守り}} bestowed by the Shinto shrines.
Ringing suzu hang under the eaves of the Shinto shrine, means like a doorbell for gods. In this case, another flat-shaped one without clapper but mallet, called {{nihongo|waniguchi|鰐口}}(literally crocodile mouth) is also used.
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Bells]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[ja:鈴]]

{{otheruses|Suzu (disambiguation)}} [[Image:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|Suzu hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]] A {{nihongo|'''''suzu'''''|鈴}} is a ball-shaped [[Shinto]] religious bell. Ringing a handheld clustered ''suzu'' like [[Jingle bell|sleigh bells for musical instrument]], is the same as waving a {{nihongo|''[[gohei]]''|御幣}}. It means calling gods and divinized heroes. Because it call them, the bell gets their spiritual power. Through its use, people thus acquire authority and power, and evil is repelled.
The ''suzu'' itself came to be believed to have spiritual power, so a small one became a bell charm called {{nihongo|[[omamori]]|お守り}}, on sale at Shinto shrines.
There exists also another type of bell used in shrines, flat and without a clapper, which is rung using a mallet and is called {{nihongo|waniguchi|鰐口}} (literally "crocodile mouth").
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Bells]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[ja:鈴]]

{{otheruses|Suzu (disambiguation)}} [[Image:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|Suzu hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]] A {{nihongo|'''''suzu'''''|鈴}} is a ball-shaped [[Shinto]] religious bell. Ringing a handheld clustered ''suzu'' like [[Jingle bell|sleigh bells for musical instrument]], is the same as waving a {{nihongo|''[[gohei]]''|御幣}}. It means calling gods and divinized heroes. Because it call them, the bell gets their spiritual power. Through its use, people thus acquire authority and power, and evil is repelled.
The ''suzu'' itself came to be believed to have spiritual power, so a small one became a bell charm called {{nihongo|[[omamori]]|お守り}}, on sale at Shinto shrines.
There exists also another type of bell used in shrines, flat and without a clapper, which is rung using a mallet and is called {{nihongo|waniguchi|鰐口}} (literally "crocodile mouth").
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Bells]] [[Category:Articles lacking sources (Erik9bot)]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[ja:鈴]]

revision.text:
{{Other uses|Suzu (disambiguation)}} {{Unreferenced stub|auto=yes|date=December 2009}}
[[File:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|Suzu hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]] A {{Nihongo|'''''Suzu'''''|鈴}} is a Japanese word that refers to a bell, particularly one that is round, hollow, and containing pellets that sound the bell when agitated, similar if not exactly equivalent to a [[Jingle bell|jingle bell]]. Suzu come in various sizes, ranging from tiny ones that can be found on mobile phone straps and good luck charms, to large ones that can be found at [[Shinto]] shrines.
== Role in Shinto == ''Suzu'' of different sizes are used in [[Shinto]]. At shrines, large ''suzu'' are often found in front of shrines, as it is said that ringing them alerts the gods. Handheld clustered ''suzu'', like [[Jingle bell|jingle bells]], are often used in Shinto ceremonies. It it is said that ringing the bells calls gods, divinized heroes and spirits. It is said that by ringing suzu, people acquire authority and positive power, and evil is repelled. The ''suzu'' itself is believed to have spiritual power, and so small suzu bell charms called {{Nihongo|[[omamori]]|お守り}} are offered at Shinto shrines.
== Trivia == ''Suzu'' is also an uncommon female name in Japan meaning "Little Bell" or "Tin", representing spiritual power that is gained through the Shinto-related ''Suzu'' bell.
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Bells]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[ja:鈴]]

revision.comment: Undid revision 433170719 by [[Special:Contributions/1.33.125.131|1.33.125.131]] ([[User talk:1.33.125.131|talk]]) There's no reason to capitalize''omamori'', it's not a proper name
/* Role in Shinto */ "divinized" is incorrect English, and this is a poor description of what a kami is anyway; better to link to the article explaining it in detail

revision.text:
[[File:Suzu MET DP353272.jpg|alt=17th century Suzu from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art|thumb|17th century ''[[Kagura suzu]]'' from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art]] {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Other uses|Suzu (disambiguation)}} {{Unreferenced|date=December 2009}} [[File:浦安ノ舞(後ろ姿).jpg|thumb|Shinto priestess with ''suzu'' bells in her right hand]] [[File:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|''Suzu'' hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]]
{{Nihongo|'''''Suzu'''''|[[wiktionary:鈴|鈴]]}} is a round, hollow Japanese [[Shinto]] [[bell]] that contains pellets that sound when agitated. They are somewhat like a [[jingle bell]] in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. ''Suzu'' come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo|[[omamori]]|お守り}}) to large ones at shrine entrances.
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large ''Suzu'' drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls [[kami]], allowing one to [[Bells#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|while repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered ''Suzu'', similar to [[jingle bell]]s, are used musically at [[Shinto]] ceremonies.
In [[Edo Castle]] the larger corridor to the ''[[Ōoku]]'' which only the ''[[Shōgun]]'' as the only male was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo|''Osuzu Rōka''|御鈴廊下||large corridor of the bells}}, derived from the ringing of the ''suzu'' bells to announce his entrance.
''Suzu'' were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == ''Suzu'' is also a female name in Japan meaning "Bell" or "Tin". The kanji for ''suzu'' is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname ''[[Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]]'', meaning "bell and tree".
==See also== * [[Kagura suzu]]
{{Bells}}
{{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Bells (instrument)]] [[Category:Japanese metalwork]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{short description|Type of bell used in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Suzu MET DP353272.jpg|alt=17th century Suzu from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art|thumb|17th century ''[[Kagura suzu]]'' from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art]] [[File:浦安ノ舞(後ろ姿).jpg|thumb|''[[Miko]]'' with ''suzu'' bells in her right hand]] [[File:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|''Suzu'' hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]]
{{Nihongo|'''''Suzu'''''|[[wiktionary:鈴|鈴]]}} is a round, hollow Japanese [[Shinto]] [[bell]] that contains pellets that sound when agitated. They are somewhat like a [[jingle bell]] in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. ''Suzu'' come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo|[[omamori]]|お守り}}) to large ones at shrine entrances. It is, however, classified as a small bell since big bells are referred to as ''kane''.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com.ph/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&pg=PT28&dq=suzu+bell&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwjbisSrtNrkAhVH-2EKHZrLDQEQ6AEIKjAA#v=onepage&q=suzu%20bell&f=false|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large ''Suzu'' drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls [[kami]], allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|while repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered ''Suzu'', similar to [[jingle bell]]s, are used musically at [[Shinto]] ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with suzu bells such as those with some sort of short blade at its center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|year=|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|year=|isbn=0974261866|location=|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[Edo Castle]] the larger corridor to the ''[[Ōoku]]'' which only the ''[[Shōgun]]'' as the only male was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo|''Osuzu Rōka''|御鈴廊下||large corridor of the bells}}, derived from the ringing of the ''suzu'' bells to announce his entrance.
''Suzu'' were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == ''Suzu'' is also a female name in Japan meaning "Bell" or "Tin". The kanji for ''suzu'' is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname ''[[Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]]'', meaning "bell and tree".
==See also== * [[Kagura suzu]] * [[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]

It is, however, classified as a small bell since big bells are referred to as ''kane''.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com.ph/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&pg=PT28&dq=suzu+bell&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwjbisSrtNrkAhVH-2EKHZrLDQEQ6AEIKjAA#v=onepage&q=suzu%20bell&f=false|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large ''Suzu'' drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls [[kami]], allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|while repelling evil]]. In [[Edo Castle]] the larger corridor to the ''[[Ōoku]]'' which only the ''[[shōgun]]'' as the only male was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo|''Osuzu Rōka''|御鈴廊下||large corridor of the bells}}, derived from the ringing of the ''suzu'' bells to announce his entrance.
''Suzu'' were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.

They are somewhat like a [[jingle bell]] in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. ''Suzu'' come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo|[[omamori]]|お守り}}) to large ones at shrine entrances. It is, however, classified as a small bell since big bells are referred to as ''kane''.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&pg=PT28&dq=suzu+bell#q=suzu%20bell|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large ''Suzu'' drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls [[kami]], allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|while repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered ''Suzu'', similar to [[jingle bell]]s, are used musically at [[Shinto]] ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with suzu bells such as those with some sort of short blade at its center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[Edo Castle]] the larger corridor to the ''[[Ōoku]]'' which only the ''[[shōgun]]'' as the only male was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo|''Osuzu Rōka''|御鈴廊下||large corridor of the bells}}, derived from the ringing of the ''suzu'' bells to announce his entrance.
''Suzu'' were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == ''Suzu'' is also a female name in Japan meaning "Bell" or "Tin". The kanji for ''suzu'' is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname ''[[Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]]'', meaning "bell and tree".
==See also== * [[Kagura suzu]] * [[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]

At [[Shinto]] shrines, large {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], while [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered {{transliteration|ja|suzu}}, similar to jingle bells, are used musically at Shinto ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[Edo Castle]], the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|[[Ōoku]]}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|[[shōgun]]}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname [[Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]], meaning "bell and tree".

{{short description|Type of bell used in Shinto}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Suzu MET DP353272.jpg|alt=17th century Suzu from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art|thumb|17th century {{transliteration|ja|[[kagura suzu]]}} from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art]] [[File:浦安ノ舞 (後ろ姿).jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|[[Miko]]}} with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells in her right hand]] [[File:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]]
{{Nihongo||[[wiktionary:鈴|鈴]]|'''Suzu'''}} are round, hollow Japanese [[Shinto]] [[bell]]s that contains pellets that sound when agitated. They are somewhat like a [[jingle bell]] in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo||お守り|[[omamori]]}}) to large ones at shrine entrances. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} are, however, classified as small bells, since big bells are referred to as {{transliteration|ja|kane}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&dq=suzu+bell&pg=PT28|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], while [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered {{transliteration|ja|suzu}}, similar to jingle bells, are used musically at Shinto ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[Edo Castle]], the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|[[Ōoku]]}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|[[shōgun]]}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname [[Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]], meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.

{{short description|Type of bell used in Shinto}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Suzu MET DP353272.jpg|alt=17th century Suzu from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art|thumb|17th century {{transliteration|ja|[[kagura suzu]]}} from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art]] [[File:浦安ノ舞 (後ろ姿).jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|[[Miko]]}} with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells in her right hand]] [[File:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]]
{{Nihongo||[[wiktionary:鈴|鈴]]|'''Suzu'''}} are round, hollow Japanese [[Shinto]] [[bell]]s that contains pellets that sound when agitated. They are somewhat like a [[jingle bell]] in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo||お守り|[[omamori]]}}) to large ones at shrine entrances. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} are, however, classified as small bells, since big bells are referred to as {{transliteration|ja|kane}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&dq=suzu+bell&pg=PT28|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], while [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered {{transliteration|ja|suzu}}, similar to jingle bells, are used musically at Shinto ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[Edo Castle]], the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|[[Ōoku]]}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|[[shōgun]]}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname [[Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]], meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[Kagura suzu]]}} * {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}

There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[Edo Castle]], the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|[[Ōoku]]}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|[[shōgun]]}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname [[Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]], meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.

{{short description|Type of bell used in Shinto}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Suzu MET DP353272.jpg|alt=17th century Suzu from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art|thumb|17th century {{transliteration|ja|[[kagura suzu]]}} from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art]] [[File:浦安ノ舞 (後ろ姿).jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|[[Miko]]}} with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells in her right hand]] [[File:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]]
{{Nihongo||[[wiktionary:鈴|鈴]]|'''Suzu'''}} are round, hollow Japanese [[Shinto]] [[bell]]s that contains pellets that sound when agitated. They are somewhat like a [[jingle bell]] in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo||お守り|[[omamori]]}}) to large ones at shrine entrances. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} are, however, classified as small bells, since big bells are referred to as {{transliteration|ja|kane}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&dq=suzu+bell&pg=PT28|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], while [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered {{transliteration|ja|suzu}}, similar to jingle bells, are used musically at Shinto ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[Edo Castle]], the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|[[Ōoku]]}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|[[shōgun]]}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname [[Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]], meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[Kagura suzu]]}} * {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}

There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[Edo Castle]], the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|[[Ōoku]]}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|[[shōgun]]}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname [[Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]], meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[Kagura suzu]]}} * {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}

revision.text:
{{short description|Type of bell used in Shinto}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Suzu MET DP353272.jpg|alt=17th century Suzu from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art|thumb|17th century {{transliteration|ja|[[kagura suzu]]}} from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art]] [[File:浦安ノ舞 (後ろ姿).jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|[[Miko]]}} with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells in her right hand]] [[File:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]]
{{Nihongo||[[wiktionary:鈴|鈴]]|'''Suzu'''}} are round, hollow Japanese [[Shinto]] [[bell]]s that contains pellets that sound when agitated. They are somewhat like a [[jingle bell]] in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo||お守り|[[omamori]]}}) to large ones at shrine entrances. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} are, however, classified as small bells, since big bells are referred to as {{transliteration|ja|kane}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&dq=suzu+bell&pg=PT28|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], while [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered {{transliteration|ja|suzu}}, similar to jingle bells, are used musically at Shinto ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[Edo Castle]], the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|[[Ōoku]]}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|[[shōgun]]}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname [[Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]], meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[Kagura suzu]]}} * {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}

At [[Shinto]] shrines, large {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], while [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered {{transliteration|ja|suzu}}, similar to jingle bells, are used musically at Shinto ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]]}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname [[:en:Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]], meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagura suzu|Kagura suzu]]}} * {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}

* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagura suzu|Kagura suzu]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis 2]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suzu (bell)]][[en:Suzu (bell)]][[mk:Сузу]][[vi:Suzu]]
{{short description|Type of bell used in Shinto}}
{{Italic title}}
[[File:Suzu MET DP353272.jpg|alt=17th century Suzu from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art|thumb|17th century {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagura suzu|Kagura suzu]]}} from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art]]
[[File:浦安ノ舞 (後ろ姿).jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|[[Miko]]}} with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells in her right hand]]
[[File:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]]

{{Nihongo|||'''Suzu'''}} are round, hollow Japanese [[Shinto]] [[:en:Bell|Bell]]s that contains pellets that sound when agitated. They are somewhat like a [[:en:Jingle bell|Jingle bell]] in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo||お守り|[[:en:Omamori|Omamori]]}}) to large ones at shrine entrances. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} are, however, classified as small bells, since big bells are referred to as {{transliteration|ja|kane}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&dq=suzu+bell&pg=PT28|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />  

At [[Shinto]] shrines, large {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], while [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered {{transliteration|ja|suzu}}, similar to jingle bells, are used musically at Shinto ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>

In [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]]}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.

{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.

== Other references ==
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname [[:en:Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]], meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.

==See also==
* {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagura suzu|Kagura suzu]]}}
* {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suzu (bell)]][[en:Suzu (bell)]][[mk:Сузу]][[vi:Suzu]] {{short description|Type of bell used in Shinto}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Suzu MET DP353272.jpg|alt=17th century Suzu from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art|thumb|17th century {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagura suzu|Kagura suzu]]}} from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art]] [[File:浦安ノ舞 (後ろ姿).jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|[[Miko]]}} with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells in her right hand]] [[File:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]]
{{Nihongo|||'''Suzu'''}} are round, hollow Japanese [[Shinto]] [[:en:Bell|Bell]]s that contains pellets that sound when agitated. They are somewhat like a [[:en:Jingle bell|Jingle bell]] in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo||お守り|[[:en:Omamori|Omamori]]}}) to large ones at shrine entrances. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} are, however, classified as small bells, since big bells are referred to as {{transliteration|ja|kane}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&dq=suzu+bell&pg=PT28|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], while [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered {{transliteration|ja|suzu}}, similar to jingle bells, are used musically at Shinto ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]]}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname [[:en:Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]], meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagura suzu|Kagura suzu]]}} * {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}
==References== {{Reflist}}

{{Nihongo|||'''Suzu'''}} are round, hollow Japanese [[Shinto]] [[:en:Bell|Bell]]s that contains pellets that sound when agitated. They are somewhat like a [[:en:Jingle bell|Jingle bell]] in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo||お守り|[[:en:Omamori|Omamori]]}}) to large ones at shrine entrances. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} are, however, classified as small bells, since big bells are referred to as {{transliteration|ja|kane}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&dq=suzu+bell&pg=PT28|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], while [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered {{transliteration|ja|suzu}}, similar to jingle bells, are used musically at Shinto ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ōoku|Ōoku]]}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname [[:en:Suzuki (disambiguation)|Suzuki]], meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagura suzu|Kagura suzu]]}} * {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}

There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}, the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Ōoku|en|Ōoku|WD=Q1064983|lt=Ōoku}}}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname {{ill|Suzuki (disambiguation)|en|Suzuki (disambiguation)|WD=Q224928|lt=Suzuki}}, meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Kagura suzu|en|Kagura suzu|WD=Q954904|lt=Kagura suzu}}}} * {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}

{{Nihongo|||'''Suzu'''}} are round, hollow Japanese [[Shinto]] {{ill|Bell|en|Bell|WD=Q101401|lt=Bell}}s that contains pellets that sound when agitated. They are somewhat like a {{ill|Jingle bell|en|Jingle bell|WD=Q956406|lt=Jingle bell}} in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo||お守り|{{ill|Omamori|en|Omamori|WD=Q1756788|lt=Omamori}}}}) to large ones at shrine entrances. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} are, however, classified as small bells, since big bells are referred to as {{transliteration|ja|kane}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&dq=suzu+bell&pg=PT28|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], while [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered {{transliteration|ja|suzu}}, similar to jingle bells, are used musically at Shinto ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}, the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Ōoku|en|Ōoku|WD=Q1064983|lt=Ōoku}}}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname {{ill|Suzuki (disambiguation)|en|Suzuki (disambiguation)|WD=Q224928|lt=Suzuki}}, meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Kagura suzu|en|Kagura suzu|WD=Q954904|lt=Kagura suzu}}}} * {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}
==References== {{Reflist}}

{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.

The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname {{ill|Suzuki (disambiguation)|en|Suzuki (disambiguation)|WD=Q224928|lt=Suzuki|1=Suzuki (disambiguation)}}, meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Kagura suzu|en|Kagura suzu|WD=Q954904|lt=Kagura suzu|1=Kagura suzu}}}} * {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suzu (bell)]][[en:Suzu (bell)]][[mk:Сузу]][[vi:Suzu]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suzu (bell)]][[en:Suzu (bell)]][[mk:Сузу]][[vi:Suzu]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Suzu (bell)]][[mk:Сузу]][[vi:Suzu]] {{short description|Type of bell used in Shinto}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Suzu MET DP353272.jpg|alt=17th century Suzu from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art|thumb|17th century {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Kagura suzu|en|Kagura suzu|WD=Q954904|lt=Kagura suzu|1=Kagura suzu}}}} from Miwa, Nara Prefecture, Japan, at the Metropolitan Museum of Art]] [[File:浦安ノ舞 (後ろ姿).jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|[[Miko]]}} with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells in her right hand]] [[File:Japanese shrine Bell01.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} hanging under the eaves of a Shinto shrine]]
{{Nihongo|||'''Suzu'''}} are round, hollow Japanese [[Shinto]] {{ill|Bell|en|Bell|WD=Q101401|lt=Bell|1=Bell}}s that contains pellets that sound when agitated. They are somewhat like a {{ill|Jingle bell|en|Jingle bell|WD=Q956406|lt=Jingle bell|1=Jingle bell}} in form, though the materials produce a coarse, rolling sound. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} come in many sizes, ranging from tiny ones on good luck charms (called {{Nihongo||お守り|{{ill|Omamori|en|Omamori|WD=Q1756788|lt=Omamori|1=Omamori}}}}) to large ones at shrine entrances. {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} are, however, classified as small bells, since big bells are referred to as {{transliteration|ja|kane}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tIUECwAAQBAJ&dq=suzu+bell&pg=PT28|title=Japan And Things Japanese|last=Joya|date=2017-07-12|publisher=Routledge|isbn=9781136221866|language=en}}</ref> The former is associated with Shinto and shrines while the latter is related to Buddhist temples and ceremonies.<ref name=":0" />
At [[Shinto]] shrines, large {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} drape over entrances, as it is said that ringing them calls {{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}, allowing one to [[Bell#Bells in Japanese religion|acquire positive power and authority]], while [[Church bell#Exorcism of demons|repelling evil]]. Handheld clustered {{transliteration|ja|suzu}}, similar to jingle bells, are used musically at Shinto ceremonies. There are ceremonies, for instance, where female performers dance with {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells such as those with some sort of short blade at their center.<ref>{{Cite book|title=A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine|last=Nelson|first=John K.|date=1996|publisher=University of Washington Press|isbn=9780295975009|location=Seattle, WA|pages=[https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96 96]|url=https://archive.org/details/yearinlifeofs00nels/page/96}}</ref> The bell's cool tinkles are also considered psychological air-conditioning for the summer since their clear ringing is considered cool and refreshing.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Cherry Blossom Epiphany: The Poetry and Philosophy of a Flowering Tree|last=Gill|first=Robin D.|date=2006|publisher=Paraverse Press|isbn=0974261866|pages=187}}</ref>
In {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}, the larger corridor to the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Ōoku|en|Ōoku|WD=Q1064983|lt=Ōoku|1=Ōoku}}}}, which only the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}}} was allowed to enter, was called {{nihongo3|large corridor of the bells|御鈴廊下|Osuzu Rōka}}, derived from the ringing of the {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} bells to announce his entrance.
{{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} were traditionally made by metal craft artisans. With the onset of industrialisation, they were made by machines. The ones produced by hand however are still considered of higher quality due to the richer and more melodious sound.
== Other references == {{transliteration|ja|Suzu}} is also a female name in Japan, meaning "bell" or "tin". The kanji for {{transliteration|ja|suzu}} is often used to form a compound name, such as the well-known surname {{ill|Suzuki (disambiguation)|en|Suzuki (disambiguation)|WD=Q224928|lt=Suzuki|1=Suzuki (disambiguation)}}, meaning "bell tree" – the bell with the thick rope hanging down almost to the floor and looking like a tree trunk.
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Kagura suzu|en|Kagura suzu|WD=Q954904|lt=Kagura suzu|1=Kagura suzu}}}} * {{transliteration|ja|[[Wind chime#Eastern and Southern Asia|Fūrin]]}}
